title: Re-covering a padded storage bench
subtitle: Strip the old fabric, replace the foam if needed, and re-staple a new cover to a padded ottoman or storage bench
excerpt: How to re-cover a padded storage bench or ottoman with new upholstery fabric. Covers removing the old cover, assessing and replacing the foam, cutting the new fabric, and stapling the cover with even tensioned folds at the corners.
sourceNotes: Upholstery: A Beginner's Guide, Alex Law (2010). City and Guilds Level 2 Upholstery. Traditional UK upholstery trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The even pull applied to upholstery fabric as it is stapled or tacked to a frame. Correct tension is firm enough to remove all wrinkles from the face of the fabric but not so hard that it distorts the pattern or strains the weave. Pull opposite sides alternately (front/back, then left/right) rather than working around the perimeter in one direction — this keeps the tension even and prevents one side pulling the fabric out of position.

content.text: A padded storage bench or ottoman is one of the easiest upholstery re-covers: the shape is a simple rectangle, the foam sits on a flat board, and the fabric wraps underneath and is stapled to the base. There are no curved edges, no piped seams, and no buttons unless you choose to add them. The whole job takes 2 to 3 hours, including stripping the old cover.

Upholstery fabric (measure the top of the bench and add twice the bench depth plus 150 mm on each side for stapling under the base). Foam if replacing: 50 to 75 mm high-density foam for a seating bench. Dacron (polyester wadding); a 25 mm layer over the foam for a softer top. T50 staples in 10 mm. The staple gun. Upholstery shears. Upholstery staple remover. A tape measure.

Turn the bench upside down. Remove the base board or upholstered lid; it usually lifts off or is hinged. Using the staple remover, work around the underside of the lid or seat panel, levering each staple out. Remove any remaining staple legs with pliers. Peel off the old fabric, the dust cover (if present), and assess the foam: press firmly; the foam should spring back. If it stays compressed or has deteriorated to a crumble, replace it.
Step 2, cut and position the new foam and wadding

Cut new foam to the exact shape of the top panel using a sharp bread knife or electric carving knife. A rocking sawing motion gives a cleaner cut than pushing straight through. Glue the foam to the panel with upholstery contact adhesive. Apply adhesive to both surfaces, allow to tack off, then press together. Cut a layer of Dacron 50 mm larger than the foam on all four sides. Drape it over the foam and pull it down the sides. Secure it underneath the panel with a single staple on each side.
Step 3, cut and apply the new fabric

Cut the new fabric, adding 100 to 150 mm on all four sides for stapling. Lay the fabric face-down on a clean flat surface. Centre the padded panel face-down on the fabric. Pull the fabric up and over one long side of the panel, applying firm, even

, and staple at 50 mm intervals along the centre of the underside, 25 mm from the panel edge. Repeat on the opposite long side, pulling the fabric taut. Repeat for both short sides. Always work opposite sides alternately.
Step 4, fold the corners and staple

At each corner, fold the fabric neatly: pull the corner diagonally, fold one side over, then the other, creating a mitred fold on the face that lies flat. Press the fold firmly and staple through all layers. The front face should show a single diagonal fold on each corner with no wrinkles or fabric bunching. Apply a dust cover of calico or black bottom cloth across the underside, folding the edges under and stapling at 100 mm intervals; this covers the raw staples and gives a finished underside.

attrs.symptom: Wrinkles or puckering on the top face after stapling
attrs.cause: Tension applied unevenly — one side pulled harder than the opposite side.
attrs.fix: Remove staples on the tight side and re-pull the fabric, this time working from the centre outward and matching the tension to the opposite side before firing each staple.

attrs.symptom: Fabric pattern is running diagonally instead of straight
attrs.cause: Fabric was not squared to the panel before stapling.
attrs.fix: Remove staples, re-lay the fabric, and check that the weave lines run parallel to the panel edges before applying any tension.
